text: I'm Shawn, like Sanyam said. Today, I'm going to talk about how you can onboard your AI team with agent skills. But before that, you might be wondering, "Who is this guy? What I'm talking about today is basically a culmination of what I've been teaching my clients and what I've been learning on my own, just hacking and building stuff. AI in 2026 has been a big change, and even people that have been in AI for a long time may have missed this shift. And the main thing is that AI agents are finally smart enough to just do your work. So, in 2025, the story was all about coding agents, Claude Code, Cursor, Codex. You know, tools that were built for developers that maybe lived in the CLI. But what we're seeing this year, maybe I'll just call them co-working agents, are hitting the scene. Things like Claude Co-work or Codex in the desktop app. You don't need to be a coder or an engineer to leverage AI agents to get the full power of them anymore. These apps are built specifically for knowledge work. So, if you understand your business context, you understand how the work gets done, you can teach an agent how to do that without being technical. But the problem is that agents are really smart, but every conversation is like a fresh slate. So, you might say something like, "Generate a report for this past week's marketing performance." And Claude is very smart and ambitious and says, "Okay, happy to. Let me find your data and pull this together." So, it pulls things from Google Drive, pulls some sheets together, some files, maybe runs some code, does some analytics, and it says, "Here's the report." But you might look at the report and say, "Okay, this is the wrong file. The real data's in HubSpot and Google Analytics, not that spreadsheet. This goes to our leadership team as well, so it needs to be a TLDR three-bulleted list. And then the sections should be, uh, you know, pipeline, demand gen, web, campaign, callout, one line each, you know, something like that." So, you have to give it feedback. And Claude's like, "Okay, no problem." So, it goes and pulls data from HubSpot, goes and pulls data from Google Analytics, and it presents you another report. And so, this report's a lot better. It's like, "Okay, that's pretty good. Two things here. You know, last week's report's in this doc, so you can use that as a reference." But then finally, you know, Claude will give you something that is pretty aligned with what you're looking for. So, you finally got what you wanted, but it just took 30 minutes of back and forth to finally figure it out. This is great. This is like a great saving. It's faster than you doing the report yourself, but still, can we do better than this 30 minutes of back and forth every time you want to get this report done? And so, this is where agent skills come in. So, agent skills is this open standard, just like MCP is an open standard, but these are essentially reusable instructions that teach an agent your business's unique context, tools, and processes. So, without skills, we have the situation we just saw. Lots of tedious back and forth to finally get something that looks good. This all costs you time, and it also costs you precious tokens, and, you know, your API bill's going through the roof. And then every time you want a task like this done, you got to repeat it over and over again. But with skills, you just send your request to Claude or whatever agent, it'll automatically load in the relevant skill, so no copy-pasting prompts from Google Docs, and then it'll just do the task right the first time. And then that's it, you know, instead of tedious back and forth, wasting your time, wasting your money, the agent just does it right the first time. So, how do skills work? How do they actually pull this off? All a skill is is a folder with files in it. So, there's the skill.md file here, and then you can also have some folders in the skill folder. So, things like references, if there's like important domain knowledge that needs to be given to the agent, if there are specific assets like templates or PDFs or slide decks, those can live in the skill as well. And scripts, like if Claude wants to write itself some code to do some specific type of analytics, it can write a script for itself and just execute it. But the real power of skills is this idea of progressive disclosure, which is just a fancy way of saying get giving the agent the right context at the right time. So, the way skills work, so in the last slide I mentioned Claude will automatically pull that skill in whenever it's relevant. And the way it can do this is at the start of every conversation, instead of dumping all of these files into the context window, like instead of dumping all these files onto the agent, the agent will just see a short description of every single skill that you've taught it. And then if that skill is relevant, then Claude or whatever agent will pull in this full skill.md file, which is just like a text file with instructions in it. And then once Claude has that skill loaded in, it might, you know, pull in additional context as needed if it comes up. So, that's the anatomy of a skill. It's very simple, just a folder with files in it. And so, here's a concrete example. So, I have a skill that teaches Claude how to write emails like me, and it looks something like this. So, I've got the skill.md file, I've got references, I've got this like follow-up reference, I've got this like how to do discovery call confirmations, how to reply to my expert network people, how to do three-way intros. At the start of every conversation, Claude will just pull in the metadata, which looks like this, and knows what this skill does and when it should use it. If the skill is relevant, like I say, "Hey Claude, write an email for me." It's going to pull in this full context file. So, this is that skill.md file. And then if I'm saying specifically, "Write me a follow-up email." It will pull in this markdown file, which is all this text here. So, this is just like that idea of progressive disclosure in action. Okay, but looking at this, you might say, "Shaw, this is a ton of text. Like, how do you expect me to write this? Like, I'm going to spend more time writing skills than actually getting my work done." And the beauty of this is that you don't have to write skills at all. And every time you teach it how to do something, it's going to take notes for itself in that notepad. Skills are that notepad. It's just these notes that Claude is writing for itself.
